steps.dialogue: Look at these two bars with me. Which bar has a greater area shaded?

prompt.text: Which has more shaded : top, bottom, or same?

step.dialogue: Try once more. Take a moment to look at the amount shaded, not the number of pieces.
If we look carefully, [event:697b7e3c5d8f2523] both bars are the same size. Check how much of each bar is shaded, is it half of each?

metadata.description: Highlight both shaded regions showing they cover the same area

on_correct.dialogue: Right! The top bar shows [event:6972a908a4aff649] 2 out of 4 parts shaded [event:6972892ef1f01ed9] , and the bottom bar shows [event:6972895fc80ce3ea]  4 out of 8 parts [event:69728977bf49cc05]  shaded, but both bars shade the [event:697289c18200816c] same amount of space.

steps.dialogue: Let me share how I think about this. I see the top bar has [event:69728f2bf6a82e15] bigger pieces, and the [event:69728c85616d6e26] bottom one has [event:69728ec36ec33d2c] smaller pieces, but I'm not counting pieces [event:697295cce584e4ba]. I'm looking at how much of the whole bar is [event:69728c5d0793fe5c] shaded. Even though the [event:697295fbad76e3ff] bottom has more [event:6972934f7a8dddd2] parts, they don't actually cover more space [event:697294a66cdf8a95]. Both shaded sections take up the same amount of the bar. Today we're going to explore how the same amount can be named with different numbers[event:697296c696a90aa8].

Let's look at these three bars carefully. They're all the same size, but they're [event:6972a3051e50e860] divided and shaded differently. Which [event:6973f3dc4676196e] two bars show the same amount of shading?

prompt.text: Click to select two bars showing the same amount.

step.dialogue: Not quite. Take a moment to look at the amount shaded, not the number of pieces.

metadata.description: Dotted overlay lines appear showing same coverage

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] You're working on it. Here's what helps me: different numbers of pieces can show the same amount. [event:697b80e0f6074601] compare the shaded space, not the number of pieces.

metadata.description: Bars slide to align, showing matching portions

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. The top bar has [event:697b87470535ca9f] 1 out of 2 parts shaded, that's half. The middle bar has [event:697b8765028cbd91] 2 out of 4 parts shaded, also half. The bottom bar has [event:697b8782fd02f83f] 3 out of 4, that's more than half. [event:69837e32990d1ec3] The top [event:697b87adc3c52ab6] and middle bars match.

on_correct.dialogue: [event:6973c51849f799b2] Right. The top bar shows [event:6973c3a4cb24acba] 1 out of 2 [event:6973c4050cbea68c] shaded, and the middle bar shows [event:6973c418a204b137] 2 out of 4 [event:6973c4365d928789] shaded, but both bars [event:6973c62c6ae1f453] shade the [event:69837d1f4cd822b1] same amount of space.

steps.dialogue: One bar has [event:6973c850545219d3] bigger pieces, and one bar has [event:6973c89b3a32b69f] smaller pieces, but the [event:6973c8d4c4e0317a]  shaded area on each [event:6973c91427f41780] bar is the same. In the last lesson, [event:6973ca0a3fb97485] we saw how [event:6973cbc1aa87e033] different fractions can [event:6973e1ca4ea3b2a4] equal one, as long as the [event:6973cbff9d28a647] numerator and [event:6973f5217a4f00b5] denominator match. In this lesson, we'll learn how [event:6973cc434af5f9d6] other fractions can equal each other [event:6973f537f374cb6e] too, even when they [event:6973f55d70345424] don't equal 1.
Let's continue to explore how different fractions equal each other.
